text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My name's Don Felker.

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Welcome to the show.

text: This episode of Fragmented is brought to you by AndroidJobs.io.

text: On Android Jobs, you can post a job posting for free and also sign up to get weekly notification emails of new job postings.

text: Sign up to get notified of new job postings.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And thanks for listening and checking out the site.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, that's androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hey everybody, welcome back to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Today, it's just going to be a solo show with me today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I did talk to Kaushik prior to recording this to see if this is something that we thought all of you or the people out in the industry would benefit from.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And we agreed that it probably would be.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so it's going to be short and to the point.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's get right to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: with all the recent layoffs in tech from all of the various companies there's a lot of people out there that are out looking for jobs there's a lot of worry there's a lot of concern not really sure how to find a position for example
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's many people that have been at the same company for four or five, six, seven, eight, nine, 10 plus years that have been recently laid off.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And when you've been at a company that long, getting back out into the workforce is a very daunting task because a lot of things have changed since you've last interviewed or since you've been through the process.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, sure, you may have interviewed people, but being on the other side of that, being the person who's being interviewed and actually having to look for positions and compete for positions is a very stressful and difficult thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And especially if you don't know how to go find a position.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I wanted to talk today about how you can land a job after getting laid off.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this also applies if you're just trying to find a job in general.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you can apply it to both sides, but it's really timely given the current tech landscape.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, so I'm just going to hop right into it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you have been laid off or if you're out there looking for a job, the first thing that you need to do is you need to update your profile of experience on LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you do not have a LinkedIn, you need to go create one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A lot of people have disabled it over the years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just let's ignore all that advice.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Go turn your LinkedIn completely back on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't make yourself private.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Make yourself as available as possible.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: update your experience of where you've worked, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then you're going to go into LinkedIn and they have something known as a resume builder.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is something you can use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I believe it's free to use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I think mine was free.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you go in there and you update it and you build your resume.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then once you build your resume, you can actually download it, I think as a PDF or one of multiple different formats.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then you can save it locally to send to whoever you need to send it to.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's useful because now you have a resume that's
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: on LinkedIn and you can use that resume to apply to places and you can use it on LinkedIn to send to people, etc.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: OK, so you've updated your LinkedIn experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You've created your resume through the LinkedIn resume builder.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the next thing you need to do is on LinkedIn, you're going to need to go into the settings and you need to go to the open to work feature and you need to toggle yourself as open to work in the past.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What this typically will do is it'll update your profile picture and it'll include like a little badge on your profile picture.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's something green.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I remember correctly, it says open to work on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So when people see your profile picture through various different methods on LinkedIn, they'll see that you're available to work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That usually will entice them to click into your profile and learn more and perhaps reach out to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this also informs recruiters on the platform that this person, so we'll use myself as an example.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I set myself to open to work, it will let the recruiters know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: that I am available for work opportunities.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I recently went through this a number of months ago, about six months ago and did exactly this and was, I am very not, I am not active on LinkedIn really ever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I might go in there and check my connections and accept all the new connections I have.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't deny anybody.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I accept all connections and then I blog out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's all I've done on LinkedIn for probably eight, 10 years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But as soon as I enabled myself to available and open to work, my inbox became almost inundated with opportunities, we'll say.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These came from...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: recruiters that were at companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These came from tech recruiters that are at recruiting companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This came from people that were engineers at other companies that knew that they had an opening that perhaps maybe had wanted to work with me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I understand my situation might be different because I have this podcast, I have been doing videos, I have written books, so my situation is different.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I do know other people that have followed this exact protocol and had similar results.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The key here is leveraging the platform to allow it to do what it does best, which is to connect people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So when you update that LinkedIn experience and you've set yourself open to work, all of a sudden you'll start having people starting to reach out to you, given what you've done, if it's a mobile developer, web developer, or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's really step one, you know, is updating that profile on LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Step two is setting that LinkedIn status to open to work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's going to just kind of run in the background and you're just going to want to check LinkedIn a couple of times a day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I have all my notifications turned off on my phone, but I went back in the notification settings and turned them back on for LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This got annoying real fast.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, in my opinion, when you don't have a job, your number one job is to find the job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that was my full-time job at the time was to go find a job or find a client or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so these are, again, this is all experience and skills that I have used over the last 15, 20 years to maintain consulting clients, contracting clients, and full-time jobs, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I know this works because this is the exact thing that I do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it works for both consulting, contracting, as well as for full-time employment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, so we've got the first two steps there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the third step here might rub people a little bit the wrong way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But again, I know this works because it has always worked for me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that is to directly reach out to recruiters you know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, you may be thinking, but Don, I don't know any recruiters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I can't stand recruiters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I tell them to go away.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, I understand.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What I advise here is that you realize that, hey, you probably do need a little bit of help maybe, and then go back into your email or go into your LinkedIn messages and find the recruiters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do a little bit of searching.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Give yourself a couple of hours to go through there and go into your email box, find all the recruiters who have contacted you over the last couple of years, and tag them with a recruiter label in your email client of choice.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps do the same thing in LinkedIn or anywhere else you've been able to contact or interact with recruiters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then what you're going to want to do is reach out to them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you can use a very simple script.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this very simple script goes something like this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hey, so-and-so, I haven't talked to you in quite a while.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hope everything's doing well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just wanted to let you know that I'm back on the market.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My skills are currently X, Y, and Z.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm kind of looking for a role that's in one of these areas.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, I'm also open to anything else that's A, B, C, D, E.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you have anything, please let me know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'd love to chat.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can reach out to me here or here's my phone number as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Look forward to talking to you soon.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Regards, Don.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just friendly, to the point, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What you will find is a lot of recruiters will reach back out to you and say, oh, yes, I've got some opportunity over here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, let's say you're an Android developer and they say, hey, we've got this opportunity to work in Xamarin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you're like, I don't want to work in Xamarin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, you don't have to take Xamarin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or I've got this opportunity working with a Bluetooth low-energy device that connects to an Android app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And maybe you don't like Bluetooth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, well, you don't have to choose that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But what you're going to get is a lot of opportunities here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And one thing I want you to keep in mind is that, and for me, what has really worked well for me...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: is that I approach all these opportunities as I kind of weigh them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Where I'm currently sitting, do I have a client?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do I have an income source?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do I have cashflow coming in?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What's the level of, at this point in time, the urgency to land a position?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The more urgent it is, the less picky you probably get to be.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's something to keep in mind because sometimes we just got to do things to make ends meet and that's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So perhaps it's going to be something that you don't really love, but hey, at least you get your foot in the door and perhaps you can move up or use it as a way to move to another opportunity.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the same thing also goes for maybe you're looking for a full-time position, but you're having a hard time finding one, but you find a contracting position through a recruiter that's three to six months long.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, you know what?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can probably utilize that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you've been laid off recently, you probably still have some benefits for a little while.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This will kind of bridge that gap until you can find another full-time position.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So there's options out there of kind of how you can navigate this marketplace of available opportunities.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you're going to reach out to all the recruiters that you know, and you can use the same exact script for all of them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So just create the script once, copy and paste it, change the name and send it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And yes, does it sound a little robotic?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Does it sound a little repetitive?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Of course it does.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But what do recruiters do to you?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They do the same exact thing to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Recruiters are matchmakers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're matchmakers for companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're matching what the company is looking for, for the technical talent.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then once that match is made, they move on to the next one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's just the way that it works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, of course, they scrape some money off the top and they have a way to make their money, but that's just the way that the industry works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you see them as a tool to get from point A to point B, they are very valuable.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I've mentioned this many times.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: i have attested recruiters as my secret weapon to never going without work over the last 20 years i've been through recessions i have been laid off i have been everything i have not been without a position for more than 24 to 48 hours i think there's it's just amazing how much you can rely on the network of recruiters are out there to help you out when you're in a bind so i want you to keep that in mind it's reach out to those recruiters that's number three
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, number four, and I'm telling you these in order that you should do them because this is going to get you the best results.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is based on personal experience that I have doing exactly this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, number four is manual discovery.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And manual discovery is where you're going to reach out to various companies that you're interested in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to go to their careers pages.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to find that they have a position that you're interested in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you're going to submit an application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Know somebody at that company, though.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is key.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's say you go to a company that you would love to get a job at and you know someone that works there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Talk to that person.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: See if you can get your resume through that person.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's going to be a warm lead.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to be a referral and it's going to get priority treatment over a cold lead that comes in through an application page where it usually hits a HR manager's inbox before it goes to an engineering manager.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you have someone that you know at the company you want to go to, go through that person if you can, send them your resume, have them make the connection.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Your chances are much higher of moving through the process than if it's just a cold outreach.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But anyway, so if you want to reach out to whatever company you want, you can just go to the careers page and see what jobs they have and submit from there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, you can also go to, and I highly recommend this, go to job boards and apply directly through all these various different job boards if you're not sure what's out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a ton of different job boards for Android ones.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I actually run an Android job board.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So full disclaimer there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But you can go there, sign up for free job notifications and so forth and see what people are hiring for.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you can contact the companies directly through those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's known as manual discovery.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So those are the four main methods that you're going to basically get an influx of interest to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so half the time it's going to be through LinkedIn, people reaching out to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you can perhaps even post on LinkedIn saying, hey, I'm looking for a position in this technology.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have this experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can make that as a status update.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, LinkedIn is going to pick up on this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's an algorithm at LinkedIn too, and it's going to eventually start referring people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Recruiters are going to find you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, last thing about LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When a recruiter asks to connect, always accept it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I know I just probably lost half the listeners, if not more, but they're going to immediately reach out, be in your inbox saying, hey, you interested in this opportunity?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Save yourself some time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just write a quick little reply.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And here's what I have.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have this saved.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this goes, hey, blank, blank being their name.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hey, blank.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Thanks for reaching out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I really appreciate it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: At this time, I'm currently fully booked up, but that does change usually every three to six months.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So please reach out around three to six months and we'll see if we have any availability to work together, period.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Wish you the best of luck in your search.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Have a great day, Don.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have used this exact script.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I haven't memorized.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've used this exact script for over 10 years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I use it in Gmail.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I use it in LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I use it anywhere where recruiters will contact me and I accept.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Every single recruiter connection through LinkedIn and every email that I get from them, I reply with that and I label them as recruiter in my inbox and I archive it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this is really a secret sauce and I understand that a lot of you may have not been doing this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But going forward, it's a fantastic way to give yourself resilience and anti-fragility to make sure that you can weather the storms that kind of happen that are out of your control.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Because you get laid off, you're in a dire situation, you need to find a gig, you can open up your inbox, you can open up your email client, go to that tag recruiters, find them, create a draft, blast all these recruiters a message.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to have opportunities in your inbox within hours, like clockwork.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we've got the LinkedIn thing, your LinkedIn status set to open to work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're doing direct, you've reached out to the recruiters and you're doing manual discovery.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's great.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now the thing to keep in mind here, just make sure you're checking your email, maybe a couple of times a day, at least checking your LinkedIn messages a couple of times a day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You want to be quick to reply to these people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You want to be active.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't want to take a couple of days to get back to them because they're trying to fill positions.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if they're interested in you, you want to make sure that you can provide as much responsiveness as possible.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that kind of covers a high level.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, there are some ancillary things that you can do to help boost your chances of landing a gig.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's quickly talk about those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I have five listed out here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, these are in no specific order, but they will all help you land a gig.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I know this, again, through personal experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, blog about the technology you're looking to get a job in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can write about anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you write about web dev or Android dev or iOS dev or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're writing about Flutter, write about how you did something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Put it on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't matter if you don't have a blog on WordPress or anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm not a huge fan of Medium, but Medium works too.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Put it up there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just get some content out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Share it on social media.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Share it on LinkedIn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let people know that you know what you're talking about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this could be any type of technology.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I highly recommend it's what you're trying to get a job in though, because you want to demonstrate that you have experience and knowledge in that subject matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, number two, create a demo app or an application that you can use to showcase your talents and put that on GitHub and release it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's say you want to build a mobile app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Build a mobile app, release the code as open source on GitHub, maybe add some tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't have to be over-architected, but at least make sure your code is somewhat clean.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Follow decent patterns and practices.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't over architect it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe have some tests, but at least allow people to say, oh, I can see some code that Don has written here on GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It looks pretty good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You know, there's some things I might do differently, but hey, that's good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That gives the engineering manager, the engineers, whoever, HR, the recruiter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: some confidence that, hey, this person knows what they're doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if it's a mobile app, you could even take that app and release it on one of the markets.

text: And the more people that know you...

text: I think that is the key takeaway from the ancillary section here of how you can boost your chances of landing a gig.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm just going to recap those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, blog about the technology you're looking to get a job in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number two, create a demo app and use it to showcase your talents.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Put it on GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number three, record a how-to video and post it on YouTube showing how to do something like a tutorial.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number four, record or start a podcast or reach out to hosts of other podcasts and offer to share some deep knowledge that you have or talk about some soft skills or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And number five, speak at local user groups, local regional conferences.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And these are going to be where you don't have to worry about travel, flights, hotels, and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Something you can just drive to and drive home at the same day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Might be a couple of hours drive, but hey, that's doable.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You do these things, people find out about you and eventually opportunities start coming your way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But to recap the initial ones, again, update your LinkedIn profile, use the resume builder to build your resume, set your LinkedIn status to open to work, directly reach out to recruiters you know, and do some manual discovery and start applying for positions.
